title: Overflows in Fixed-point Representations and Floating-point Representations
main_topics: IV.5. Overflows in Operations on Fixed-point Representations
subtopics: Moving to longer and shorter representations
Overflow definition and detection in C2

definitions.description: Occurs when the result of an arithmetic operation falls outside the representable interval [min, max].
Extending an n-bit representation to (n+k) bits: for A+S, insert k zeros after the sign bit; for C1/C2, repeat the sign bit k times.
N = (-1)^S × 1.f × 2^(C - excess), where S is the sign, f is the fractional part of the mantissa, and C is the characteristic (biased exponent).
Floating-point form where exactly one significant digit (always 1 in base 2) is to the left of the decimal point: 1.xx...x × 2^e.
The significand of a floating-point number; in normalized form M = 1.f (only f is stored).
The biased exponent field in a floating-point number; stores e + excess.
Special floating-point value representing the result of an undefined operation (e.g., 0/0). Propagates through computations.
A floating-point value smaller in magnitude than the smallest normalized number; uses form 0.f × 2^e_min.
The exponent is too small to represent; the number is treated as 0.

formulas.name: C2 overflow condition
formulas.description: Overflow occurs iff C_{n-1} ≠ C_n (the two most-significant carry bits differ). Detected by attaching an NXOR gate to the adder.

formulas.name: IEEE 754 single precision
formulas.description: 32 bits: 1 sign + 8 exponent (excess-127) + 23 fractional mantissa. Range: ±1.2×10^(-38) to ±3.4×10^38.

formulas.name: IEEE 754 double precision
formulas.description: 64 bits: 1 sign + 11 exponent (excess-1023) + 52 fractional mantissa. Range: ±1.7×10^(-308) to ±1.7×10^308.

algorithms.name: Floating-point addition
steps: Compare the exponents; shift the mantissa of the smaller number to align
Round the result to the available bits

algorithms.name: Floating-point multiplication
steps: Determine the sign (XOR of the two sign bits)

examples.name: C2 overflow example 1
examples.description: 1111 + 1111 = carry-out, but result 1110 is correct (no overflow; C3 = C4 = 1).

examples.name: arccos(-1) with extended arithmetic
examples.description: Requires representation of ∞ to compute arccos(-1) = π through the formula.

exercises: Follow floating-point addition steps for -0.75 and +0.375 (IEEE 754 single precision)
Follow floating-point multiplication steps for the same numbers
Prove that overflow cannot occur when adding numbers of opposite signs
learning_objectives: Apply sign extension to change representation width
Detect overflow in C2 arithmetic using carry bits and NXOR
Explain the structure of IEEE 754 floating-point (single and double precision)
Encode and decode IEEE 754 single-precision numbers
Distinguish normalized, denormalized, zero, infinity, and NaN values
Perform floating-point addition and multiplication step by step
Understand the precision and rounding implications of floating-point
